subject: Gloves and gauntlets
method: full human/LLM read of transcripts 043 (Act 1) + 044 (Act 2) + 045 (Act 3), not the heuristic
note: All 71 tiers confirmed against the transcripts. The author calls gloves the most impactful item slot. Growling Underdog reflects his on-air bump to A; Winkling's caption 'B tier' is a slip (he equates it to the D-tier Unwanted Masterwork). Many passive-bonus gloves work as stat sticks. Auto-caption renderings: Hra'cknir Bracers='Rockne/herir bracers', Ichorous Gloves='icaris', Jhannyl's='janal's', Bhaalist Gloves='ballist gloves', Servitor='serator', Gloves of Uninhibited Kushigo='kuso'.

Bracers of Defence.why: +2 AC while unarmored and shieldless — effectively a shield for monks (and shield-less casters), letting them reach competitive AC; best-in-slot defensively for several strong builds and a top-2 glove for a wide array of characters early.

Daredevil Gloves.why: +1 to spell attack rolls and lets you fire ranged spells in melee without disadvantage — best-in-slot for warlocks (Eldritch Blast) and to-hit cantrip casters until save-DC gloves arrive.

Gloves of Archery.why: +2 flat damage on every ranged weapon attack (plus bow proficiency) — best-in-slot for any archer for much of Act 1, and the +2 stays relevant deep into the game; great on dual-hand-crossbow Thief Rogues.

Gloves of Cinder and Sizzle.why: +1d4 fire on every unarmed strike (and a free Scorching Ray) — the first glove that boosts unarmed damage, so best-in-slot for monks, who attack many times and crit-double the die.

Gloves of Dexterity.why: Set Dexterity to 18 — at worst a free +2 initiative/+1 attack, at best (drop Dex to 8) a free feat and a pile of stat points; the best item in the game, enabling and supercharging hundreds of builds.

Gloves of Uninhibited Kushigo.why: +1d4 damage on thrown and improvised weapons — the first glove to boost a thrower's damage, so best-in-slot for the very strong throwing-weapon builds in Act 1.

Hellrider's Pride.why: Casts Blade Ward (halve physical damage) on anyone you heal — effectively doubles a healed ally's HP for free; an Ancients paladin or AOE healer can keep the whole party warded, and you can even throw healing potions to apply it.

Wondrous Gloves.why: +1 AC for any character (stacks with armor/shield) plus an extra Bardic Inspiration use (four more per day once it refreshes on short rest) — best-in-slot for many bards and a solid AC bump for everyone else.

Dark Justiciar Gauntlets.why: +1d4 necrotic on every weapon attack — exactly what the offensive glove slot is for; the only downsides are the often-resisted necrotic type and arriving late in Act 2, but most parties happily run two damage-rider gloves.

(Rare) Dark Justiciar Gauntlets.why: The upgraded version: the same +1d4 necrotic plus a free, cooldown-less bonus-action Beckoning Darkness (2d8 necrotic, DC14 Cha) — an S-tier glove with a strong bonus-action damage option on top.

Flawed Helldusk Gloves.why: +1d4 fire on weapon attacks OR +1d4 necrotic (plus bleed) on unarmed — a flexible damage-rider almost any party wants; thrown attacks count as both, and it enables several broken prone/maim builds.

Gloves of the Automaton.why: Bonus-action self-transform granting advantage on ALL weapon attacks for 10 turns once per short rest, no condition — permanent free advantage for any non-advantage build; even better via the bug that keeps it when swapped off.

Luminous Gloves.why: Apply two stacks of Radiating Orb on any radiant damage — with one other radiant source you hit 8-10 stacks fast, shutting off an enemy's attacks entirely; core to the broken radiating-orb strategy.

Gauntlet of the Tyrant.why: +1 spell save DC, +1d4 force on unarmed, and Command at level 3 once/rest — the best endgame spellcaster gloves for landing control effects (save DC plus a free cast of one of the most-used spells).

Gloves of Soul Catching.why: The only legendary gloves: +2 Con, +1d10 force on every unarmed strike, and per-turn either heal 10 or gain near-permanent advantage on attacks/saves — absurd numbers, endgame best-in-slot for every monk.

Helldusk Gloves.why: +1d6 fire (weapon) or +1d6 necrotic/bleed (unarmed), +1 to ALL attack rolls AND +1 spell save DC — best-in-slot endgame gloves for nearly everyone (caster or martial) who doesn't need Gloves of Dexterity or Soul Catching; there's only one.

Legacy of the Masters.why: +2 to attack and damage with weapons (melee and ranged) — a massive flat boost any weapon user loves; comparable to the Helldusk Gloves and acquired a bit earlier, so it lands in most parties.

The Reviving Hands.why: Like Hellrider's Pride (Blade Ward on heal) but NOT nerfed — works on self-healing too, so any character with a healing aura/Ring of Regeneration gets near-permanent Blade Ward; plus a free Revivify.

Gloves of Baneful Striking.why: Currently bugged to apply no-save Bane (-1d4 to all saves) on any weapon hit — helps land your (and allies') control spells and reverberation/snowburst follow-ups; great on tiger barbarians/sword bards who hit many enemies.

Gloves of Belligerent Skies.why: Two stacks of Reverberation on thunder/lightning/radiant damage (works in AOE) — the key glove for reverberation builds, getting much better in later acts when those damage types are easy to apply.

Gloves of the Growling Underdog.why: Advantage on melee attacks while two or more enemies are in melee range — a strong, if positioning-dependent, early advantage source; bumped to A because the author kept recommending it over other fighter gloves.

The Sparkle Hands.why: Two lightning charges per unarmed hit (+1 to hit, +1d8 every five) — a fine alternative to Cinder and Sizzle for non-Tavern-Brawler monks who value the to-hit bonus, especially if found first.

Gloves of the Balanced Hands.why: Grants the Two-Weapon Fighting style (add your modifier to off-hand damage) — best-in-slot damage for a mono-class Thief Rogue dual-wielding, often beating even the +damage gloves; redundant if you already get the style from a class.

Hra'cknir Bracers.why: Quickened Mage Hand plus Telekinesis once per short rest — a free, repeatable level-5 spell with tons of creative uses (shove enemies off cliffs, reposition, out-of-combat traversal); great to swap in for fights where it shines.

Craterflesh Gloves.why: +1d6 (so 2d6 on the crit) force damage on critical hits, and it works on SPELL attacks — best-in-slot for a crit-focused Eldritch Blast warlock; lower average than flat-damage gloves for non-crit builds.

Dark Displacement Gloves.why: Throwing a weapon swaps your position with the hit enemy (and +1 thrown attack) — a powerful, wild repositioning tool best on a non-dedicated thrower (e.g. a dagger-tossing monk); risky for a main thrower since you don't always want enemies pulled to you.

Gauntlets of Hill Giant Strength.why: Set Strength to 23 from the glove slot; great on a no-elixir run or to free your elixir slot, but a Cloud Giant elixir (27) is simply better for most, so it's the elixir-free version of that effect.

Martial Exertion Gloves.why: A free action once/short rest for an extra attack (at the cost of ~21 self-damage, easily healed) — superb for low-attack/high-damage characters and especially Hunter Rangers, since the extra attack can be Volley or a tiger cleave.

Nimblefinger Gloves.why: +2 Dexterity for gnomes (uncapped, so up to 24 with the mirror) — a huge Dex boost usable by anyone via Disguise Self, though many prefer Gloves of Dexterity (frees feats/points) and you must stay disguised as a gnome.

Servitor of the Black Hand Gloves.why: +1d4 force on unarmed strikes (plus a free Fear) — Force is the best generic damage type, so these are the best of the '+1d4 unarmed' monk gloves to bridge the gap until Helldusk/Soul Catching.

Abyss Beckoners.why: A 30ft aura giving your summons resistance to all damage (but it drives them mad on a DC13 Wis save) — doubles the durability of mindless summons immune to madness (spiritual weapon, flaming sphere, flying ghouls); a strong but risky Summoner pick.

Gloves of Heroism.why: Grant Heroism (5 temp HP/turn + fear immunity) when you use a Channel Oath ability — decent sustain and fear protection for a paladin who channels often (e.g. Vengeance), but most paladins have better glove options.

Gloves of Power.why: +1 Sleight of Hand and a no-save (currently) Bane on a Charisma save; the Bane is better on the Baneful Striking gloves, but the +1 Sleight of Hand is a real, swap-in bonus when you already have advantage from elsewhere.

Gloves of Thievery.why: Advantage on Sleight of Hand — the go-to lockpick/pickpocket glove to swap in when you lack another advantage source (otherwise prefer Gloves of Power); functionally paired with them.

Braindrain Gloves.why: Psychic damage applies stacking Mental Fatigue (-1/turn to mental saves) — a poor man's Arcane Acuity that, with an illithid psychic-damage power, reliably lands Hold Person; useful when better save-stacking isn't available.

Gauntlets of Surging Accuracy.why: +1d4 to hit on all attacks the turn you Action Surge — a nice bonus on your most important fighter turns, but it's only once/short rest and you usually don't need help on a surge turn, so flat-damage gloves often win.

Gloves of the Duelist.why: +2 to melee attack rolls with a one-handed weapon (works with a shield) — solid hit chance for a sword-and-board character, but outclassed by the Automaton's advantage and the Growling Underdog for those builds.

Bonespike Gloves.why: Your attacks ignore bludgeoning/piercing/slashing resistance — effectively doubles your damage against the many physically-resistant Act 3 enemies; a great swap-in for those fights (unneeded if you have the Bhaalist Armour).

Gauntlets of the Warmaster.why: +1 to hit and enemies have disadvantage on saves vs your weapon maneuvers/actions — strong for a Battle Master or a Soulbreaker-Greatsword stun user, but many fighters prefer raw damage/advantage gloves.

Seraphic Pugilist Gloves.why: +1d4 radiant on unarmed strikes (plus a free Guiding Bolt) — mainly for an open-hand monk radiating-orb build (stack radiant with the Callous Glow ring); otherwise a minor upgrade over other +1d4 monk gloves.

Snow-Dusted Monastery Gloves.why: +1d4 cold on unarmed strikes (plus a free Ice Knife) — cold synergizes (Snowburst ring, Wet doubling), so a niche pick for a cold-focused monk, though endgame gloves overtake it.

Spellmight Gloves.why: A spell-version of Great Weapon Master: -5 to a spell attack for +1d8 damage (once per spell on honour mode) — best on a crit-focused Eldritch Blast warlock who ignores the to-hit penalty, though Craterflesh usually beats it. (S-tier below honour mode.)

Stalker Gloves.why: +1 initiative and +1d4 force on sneak attacks; the sneak-attack damage is small (once/round) versus per-attack gloves, so it's mainly for a non-rogue who desperately needs initiative and can spare the slot.

Unlucky Thief's Gloves.why: +2 Sleight of Hand (the largest such glove bonus) — a joke item (you accumulate charcoal and eventually catch fire from stealing) but a genuinely useful swap-in for the toughest locks.

Fleetfingers.why: Jump for free whenever you dash — extra mobility for a bonus-action-dashing Rogue, but Rogues rarely have mobility problems and the slot is better spent on stats/damage.

Gloves of Hail of Thorns.why: Hail of Thorns once/short rest as just an action (free 1d10 AOE on a hit) — fine if a weapon attacker is starved for AOE, but otherwise unexciting.

Gloves of Missile Snaring.why: Reaction to reduce one ranged attack's damage by 1d10+Dex — a defensive band-aid for a low-AC caster getting pincushioned (e.g. the goblin camp), but the Bracers of Defense or Shield spell usually do it better.

Cindersnap Gloves.why: Protection from Missiles once/short rest (near-immunity to archers for 10 turns) — a purely defensive swap-in for archer-heavy fights or the Mirror fight, but you'd rather buff offense from this slot.

Gloves of Crushing.why: +1 to hit and +2 damage on unarmed strikes — a fine effect, but slightly worse than the Sparkle Hands and badly outnumbered by better monk gloves by the time you find them.

Jhannyl's Gloves.why: +1 saves and auto-cast Lesser Restoration on yourself once/rest when blinded/poisoned/paralyzed — niche insurance for an otherwise-invincible build (Dodge tank, abjuration wizard) against the conditions that can still drop them.

Poisoner's Gloves.why: Apply Poisoned on poison damage — the core of the Derivation Cloak / Brood Mother's Revenge poison-loop combo, but it costs several gear slots for a weak payoff and many Act 2 enemies are poison-immune.

Swordmaster Gloves.why: Sword proficiencies and +1 to melee attacks; most characters already have the proficiencies and prefer damage or the better hit-chance gloves, so it rarely sees use (niche: a non-martial wanting greatsword proficiency).

Thermoarcanic Gloves.why: Build Heat on fire damage to dump as bonus fire damage later — a solution in search of a problem (boosts AOE damage late in a fight, when you don't need it), and the per-turn self-damage threatens concentration.

Bhaalist Gloves.why: +1 to hit and a once/rest Garrotte (silence + immobilize, DC14 str) that also roots YOU and costs concentration — the effect is decent but the DC is too low and the action cost too high for Act 3, so only a desperate caster-lockdown swap-in.

Gemini Gloves.why: Once/short rest your cantrip hits an extra target (stacks with Twin Spell) — a nice doubling for a dedicated cantrip build (e.g. Ray of Frost), but most casters want gloves that improve their leveled-spell turns too.

Knock Knuckle.why: Knock once per long rest — only saves a scroll/slot for a party with no Sleight of Hand character; safe to ignore otherwise.

Quickspell Gloves.why: Cast an action cantrip as a bonus action once/short rest — best for non-targeted cantrips (Blade Ward, True Strike), so a niche pick for an abjuration wizard keeping Blade Ward up in long fights.

Thunderpalm Strikers.why: +1d4 thunder on unarmed strikes (plus a free Thunderwave) — the obvious reverberation-monk glove, except the Gloves of Belligerent Skies (which apply reverb) need the same slot, so the synergy can't happen.

Leather Gloves.why: Non-magical gloves with no effect — purely cosmetic.

Metallic Gloves.why: Non-magical, +1 strength saves (medium armor) — strength saves are extremely rare, so barely an upgrade over nothing.

Herbalist's Gloves.why: Cure Poisoned on a creature you heal — very few enemies poison you and poison is easily removed otherwise, so this is a near-useless effect to discard early.

Reason's Grasp.why: 15 (very) temporary HP when a barbarian manually ENDS rage — barbarians almost never want to end rage, and the HP vanish at end of combat, so there's no reason to use it.

Winter's Clutches.why: Stacking Encrusted with Frost on cold damage (freeze-stun at 7 stacks) — but reliable cold damage is hard in Act 1, the DC12 con save is easily passed, and enemies die before you stack it; rarely effective.

Gloves of Battlemage's Power.why: Supposed to grant Arcane Acuity on condition-applying weapon hits, but currently bugged and does nothing — D until fixed (would be A-tier working).

Ichorous Gloves.why: Chance to apply a weak Noxious Fumes (1d4 acid AOE) on acid damage — acid is hard to deal regularly, the DC13 dex save is easy, and the effect is tiny; not worth the setup.

Raven Gloves.why: Find Familiar (Quill the raven) once/short rest — a level-1 spell effect in a premium slot, and it can't combine with better familiars; never worth it.

Blackguard's Gauntlets.why: +1 to hit and a once/rest level-4 Inflict Wounds (medium armor) — the spell needs a high save DC but the to-hit bonus suits martials, so it fights itself, and ~32 necrotic is worse than just attacking by Act 3.

Cerebral Citadel Gloves.why: Bless for 3 turns when you charm/frighten an enemy — by Act 3 you have Bless elsewhere, and crit-focused fear builds don't need the accuracy; a situational, weak effect.

Spell-Seeking Gloves.why: Spend sorcery points for advantage on a spell attack (1 per spell level) — far too expensive (you'd rather cast another spell with those points), and advantage is cheaper from hiding/invisibility; avoid.

Unwanted Masterwork Gloves.why: +1 to attack and +1 strength saves (medium armor) — the +1 to hit is duplicated and far exceeded by many other Act 3 gloves, so it'll essentially never be worn.

Winkling Gloves.why: +1 Sleight of Hand and +1 to attack — both effects are duplicated far better by other gloves you'll already have, so like the near-identical Unwanted Masterwork gloves it almost never sees play.
